one_line_definition: Technique of inserting an intermediate material layer between two media to maximise acoustic energy transfer across their interface.
A layer whose thickness equals one quarter of the acoustic wavelength at the operating frequency, causing constructive interference of multiple internal reflections and maximising transmission.
A piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer designed to radiate and receive through air rather than a liquid or solid coupling medium.
Acoustic attenuation expressed as energy lost per wavelength of propagation (units: Np); a material-intrinsic figure of merit independent of frequency for constant-Q materials.
Ratio (in dB) of the electrical signal received by the transducer to the signal sent, measured through the full transmit-receive path in air.
Equivalent circuit (Krimholtz-Leedom-Matthaei) model for piezoelectric transducers that accounts for finite element thickness and is used to optimise matching layer impedances for maximum bandwidth.
Thin, highly porous polymeric sheet used industrially for filtration; repurposed here as a low-impedance, low-attenuation λ/4 acoustic matching layer.
Ultra-low-density porous silica solid with acoustic impedance ~0.06–0.1 MRayl and very low attenuation, theoretically ideal for air-coupled matching but mechanically impractical above ~1 MHz.
Polymeric filtration membrane exhibiting the lowest attenuation (γ < 0.11 Np) among materials tested; best candidate for air-coupled λ/4 matching layers from 0.5–1.2 MHz.
The large ratio between acoustic impedances of two media (e.g. ~35 MRayl for PZT vs ~400 Rayl for air) that causes near-total reflection of acoustic energy at their interface.

Empirical relationship α = α₀ f^β describing how attenuation coefficient scales with frequency in porous materials; β > 1 means attenuation per wavelength increases with frequency, setting an upper usable frequency limit.
Evaluation of materials or structures without causing damage, here via air-coupled ultrasound for materials characterisation and flaw detection.
